prompt:
Restructure the-valley's documentation into three rigorously separated altitudes — premise/requirements, architecture, detailed design — AND aggressively prune premature detail. The owner's directive: the docs went into excessive technical detail too early; he is 'fine losing the excessive detail for now — it's nothing we can't recreate, and I only want to add flesh to the bones as we build out and validate.' Git history is the archive; deletion is not loss. When in doubt, CUT.

Read every existing doc first: README.md, design/roadmap.md, design/contribute.md, design/verification.md, design/integration.md, design/integrator-internals.md, design/knowledge.md, design/feedback.md, design/scenarios.md, design/openquestions.md. Match the existing voice — dry, confident, opinionated. The '"Whatever."' epigraph stays.

## Target structure (the bones)

**1. README.md — the hook (rewrite).** Roughly one page, aimed at a reader who has never heard of this. The premise ('what would source management look like if designed today, with AI agents as first-class authors?'), the pain (GitHub bundles seven concerns and does several poorly; CI is slow and YAML-shaped; PRs conflate coordination/gating/record/notification; agents are second-class), the promise (compose small tools over a durable substrate you own), honest status (design stage, roadmap linked). ZERO mechanisms — no NATS, no ref namespaces, no CUE, no JSON, no hook names. Constraints by name (open source, minimal, Nix-native, decentralized-where-possible) are fine. End with a short map of the docs. This is the pitch; make it land.

**2. design/requirements.md — NEW, short.** What the system must be, no solutions: who it's for (solo dev + AI agents first, small trusted teams later, explicitly not public-social scale in v1); the seven unbundled concerns restated as needs; the four constraints with one sentence of why each; non-goals; durability as a first-class requirement (the git data is the crown jewel — losing it is the one unrecoverable failure).

**3. design/architecture.md — NEW.** The bets and their rationale, each traced to a requirement and no deeper than rationale: git-as-event-source / 'a log, not a workflow engine'; attestation-with-revocation instead of CI-as-gate (keep the two-failure-modes trade table); the pull-based integrator instead of a pre-receive gate (keep the gate-vs-controller comparison table); review reframed as observability + feedback (keep the 'what review bundles' table); project knowledge as a typed-node graph legible to humans and agents; the one structural git invariant (only the integrator writes protected refs). Component technology choices at one-line depth each (bare git over SSH, NATS JetStream, Tessera tlog via tesseract, CUE schemas, armstrong as effectful controller, klaus for agent dispatch). The system-shape diagram from the README lives here.

**4. Detailed design — PRUNE HARD.** The rule: layer-3 detail earns its place only if the CURRENT or NEXT roadmap phase (Phase 0–2: hosting/durability, event log, attestations) actually needs it. Each later phase re-fleshes its design docs when it starts, informed by what validation taught us. Concretely:
- **DELETE design/integrator-internals.md** (pure Phase-3+ internals: staleness taxonomy, queue mechanics, crash recovery — recreate when Phase 3 starts).
- **SLIM design/contribute.md** to the protocol's essence (the 4 properties + the 8 steps at one line each + the atomic-push idea + the one-invariant note); drop the JSON shapes and deep mechanics.
- **SLIM design/verification.md** to: the reframe pointer (now in architecture.md), pure-vs-effectful checks distinction, and the mechanism-stack table at most; drop the attestation-shape sketch and subscriber catalogue.
- **FOLD design/integration.md, design/feedback.md, design/knowledge.md into architecture.md** (their architecture-level content) and DELETE the remainder unless a slim residual doc genuinely earns Phase-0–2 keep; call the judgment per-doc and justify it in the PR description.
- **KEEP design/scenarios.md** — it's validation reasoning, the right kind of bones — but slim each scenario to its flow diagram + 2-3 stress bullets, fix the section numbering (currently 1,2,3,5,4,6 → sequential), and fix references to scenario numbers elsewhere.
- **KEEP design/roadmap.md** — already the right altitude. Re-point its links at requirements.md/architecture.md; where it links a doc that was deleted or folded, update or drop the link. Do not change phases, gates, or exit criteria.
- **PRUNE design/openquestions.md** — keep requirement- and architecture-level questions and near-term (Phase 0–2) design questions; drop hyper-specific questions attached to deleted detail (e.g. integrator queue minutiae, knowledge-graph ID/indexer specifics) — they'll be re-asked when their phase starts. Tag each surviving item by layer ([requirements]/[architecture]/[design]). Keep the Resolved section.

## Hard rules
- **No design changes.** Restructure and prune only — do not alter, fix, or editorialize any technical claim that survives. Cutting is fine; rewriting decisions is not.
- All relative links across all docs must resolve after the moves and deletions. Check every link, including roadmap's anchor links.
- The corpus should end up MUCH smaller. If the diff isn't strongly net-negative in lines, you've kept too much.
- **PR description must list every deletion and fold** (per-doc: kept / slimmed / folded-into-X / deleted), with a one-line note that everything remains recoverable in git history — this is the owner's review surface for vetoing any specific cut. Note anything whose altitude or keep/cut call was ambiguous.
- No AI/assistant attribution, no Co-Authored-By.

Open a PR.
